---
title: "HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    css: styles.css
    code-fold: false
lightbox: true
---

![ALMA image of the protoplanetary disk around HD 163296 showing prominent ring–gap substructures in the dust continuum. The disk exhibits several dust rings and gaps associated with pressure structures in the disk material. Image credit: ALMA (ESO/NAOJ/NRAO).](images/hd_163296_ficha.jpg){#fig-hd163296 fig-align="center" width="650"}

As shown in @fig-hd163296, the HD 163296 disk exhibits multiple dark gaps and bright rings in the millimeter dust continuum emission.

## Object names

| Catalog | Name | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Main name | HD 163296 | [@isella2016] |
| Other designations | MWC 275 | [@benisty2010] |
| Location | Not specified in these papers | — |

## Location

HD 163296 is a young **Herbig Ae star** located at a distance of approximately **101–122 parsecs (~330–400 light-years)** from Earth [@isella2016; @rab2020].

The star belongs to the class of intermediate-mass pre–main sequence stars and is surrounded by one of the most extensively studied protoplanetary disks. Because the disk is large, bright, and relatively isolated, it provides an ideal laboratory for investigating the processes that shape planet-forming disks.

## General description of the system

HD 163296 is a protoplanetary disk surrounding a Herbig Ae star whose dust morphology has made it a benchmark system for the study of radial substructures; indeed, it has been described as “**the third system known to host multiple rings in dust emission at millimetre wavelength**” [@diep2019]. On global scales, the system combines a large, dynamically ordered gas disk—“**The gas disc is in Keplerian motion and has a radius of about 550 au, and the millimetre continuum emission from solid particles is confined to within 250 au of the star**”—with a more compact solid component, where earlier observations resolved “**three concentric gap-ring pairs**,” with gaps at ~54, ~100, and 160 au [@diep2019]. Higher-resolution observations further show that this architecture is not limited to simple concentric rings: “**The continuum observations resolve and allow us to characterize the previously discovered dust rings at radii of 68 and 100. They also reveal new small-scale structures, such as a dark gap at 10 au, a bright ring at 15 au, a dust crescent at a radius of 55 au, and several fainter azimuthal asymmetries**” [@isella2018]. In this sense, the dust distribution is not uniform but exhibits both radial and azimuthal variations, since “**The dust continuum emission is characterized by several asymmetric structures**,” and these features “**corroborate the hypothesis that the complex structure of the HD 163296 disk is the result of the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets**” [@isella2018].

## General system properties

| Property | Value | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Distance | 101 ± 2 pc | Distance from Earth to the HD 163296 system. | [@pinte2020_dsharp] |
| Evolutionary class | Herbig Ae | Evolutionary classification of the central star. | [@isella2018] |
| Stellar mass | 2.04 M☉ | Mass adopted for the central star in the kinematic analysis. | [@pinte2020_dsharp] |
| Gas disk size | >1000 au diameter | Approximate full diameter of the extended Keplerian gas disk. | [@isella2018] |
| CO extent | ~560 au | Radial extent of the CO emission from the central star. | [@isella2018] |
| Disk inclination | 46.7° | Inclination of the disk used to interpret the CO kinematics. | [@pinte2020_dsharp] |
| Position angle | 133.3 ± 0.1° | Orientation of the disk major axis on the sky, derived from the outer dust rings. | [@isella2018] |
| Bright dust rings | ~15, 67, 101 au | Main bright rings resolved in the 1.25 mm dust continuum. | [@isella2018] |
| Main dark gaps | ~10, 45, 86, 141 au | Main dark gaps identified in the dust continuum emission. | [@isella2018] |
| Dust crescents | ~4 and 55 au | Prominent asymmetric dust crescent structures seen in the continuum emission. | [@isella2018] |

## Disk morphology

The morphology of HD 163296 can be described as that of a dust disk with a hierarchical architecture of rings and gaps: in the 1.3 mm continuum, the disk shows “**three concentric gap-ring pairs**,” and at higher resolution it presents “**much deeper gaps**” between the rings, more clearly defining the radial redistribution of dust [@diep2019]. To this global structure, @isella2018 add “**three new morphological features**,” including an inner dark gap at ~10 au, a bright ring at ~15 au, and a central azimuthal asymmetry, while in the outer disk “**the outermost features B67, D86, B100, D145, and B155, are well described by concentric circles**.” Beyond this concentric organization, the disk is not perfectly uniform: “**the image of the residuals shows fainter asymmetries along the B67 and B100 dust rings**” [@isella2018], and @diep2019 further conclude that the outer gap and outer ring display a “**significant modulation of their morphology and intensity**.” Altogether, HD 163296 exhibits a morphology in which gaps, rings, and local asymmetries coexist within a globally thin and well-organized disk structure.

## Morphology measurements

| Structure | Measurement | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disk geometry | Inclination = 46.7 ± 0.1°; PA = 133.3 ± 0.1° | Mean geometry derived from the outer concentric structures. | [@isella2018] |
| D10 | Radius = 9.96 ± 0.07 au | Inner dark gap in the 1.25 mm continuum; its lower apparent inclination is likely affected by beam smearing. | [@isella2018] |
| B14 | Radius = 14.44 ± 0.07 au (ellipse fit); 15.5 ± 0.2 au (Gaussian model); width = 8.7 ± 0.2 au | Inner bright ring just outside D10; together with D10, it is spatially resolved mainly along the disk major axis. | [@isella2018] |
| D45 | Radius = 44.77 ± 0.19 au | Dark gap in the inner outer-disk region; its morphology may be partly affected by the dust crescent at ~55 au. | [@isella2018] |
| B67 | Radius = 66.99 ± 0.11 au (ellipse fit); 67.08 ± 0.03 au (Gaussian model); width = 6.56 ± 0.05 au; residual asymmetry = ±0.15 mJy beam⁻¹ | Bright outer ring; shows real azimuthal substructure beyond beam-scale noise. | [@isella2018] |
| D86 | Radius = 86.61 ± 0.22 au | Dark gap between the two main outer bright rings. | [@isella2018] |
| B100 | Radius = 99.69 ± 0.08 au (ellipse fit); 101.16 ± 0.04 au (Gaussian model); width = 5.8 ± 0.1 au; fainter asymmetries detected in residual map | Second major bright outer ring in the dust continuum. | [@isella2018] |
| D141 | Radius = 140.62 ± 0.96 au | Outer dark gap identified in the continuum map. | [@isella2018] |
| B159 | Radius = 158.7 ± 1.2 au | Outermost bright ring detected in the continuum map. | [@isella2018] |
| C4 | Radius ≈ 4 au; PA = −44°; peak intensity = 0.55 mJy beam⁻¹; azimuthal extent ≈ 180° | Innermost crescent-like asymmetry, overlapping the innermost Gaussian ring component. | [@isella2018] |
| C55 | Radius ≈ 55 au; PA = 99°; peak intensity = 0.64 mJy beam⁻¹; intrinsic widths = 2.2 au radial and 16.9 au azimuthal | Prominent dust crescent inside the D45 region. | [@isella2018] |

## Dust in the disk

In the case of HD 163296, dust dynamics—that is, the radial migration of solids toward local gas-pressure maxima—is interpreted as a key mechanism in the formation of the rings. These bright rings—“**the rings B67 and B100 of the HD 163296 disk, have a radial width that is narrower than the estimated pressure scale height of the gaseous disk**” [@isella2018]—suggest that the dust is concentrated and confined within relatively narrow regions. In addition to ring and gap structures, the disk also shows asymmetric dust concentrations: “**new small-scale structures, such as a dark gap at 10 au, a bright ring at 15 au, a dust crescent at a radius of 55 au, and several fainter azimuthal asymmetries**” [@isella2018]. Among these asymmetries, the most prominent correspond to crescents, since “**The dust continuum emission is characterized by several asymmetric structures. The most prominent consist in crescents centered at about 4 and 55 au**” [@isella2018]. For this reason, the dusty environment of the disk should be understood as dynamic rather than perfectly axisymmetric. This interpretation is further reinforced by the kinematic evidence presented by @pinte2020_dsharp, who note that “**upon deprojection, we find that all of the candidate planets lie within a gap and/or at the end of a spiral detected in dust continuum emission**.” In other words, the dust substructures not only describe where solid material is concentrated or redistributed, but also spatially coincide with possible embedded perturbers, supporting the idea that part of the observed dust architecture is being sculpted by forming protoplanets.

## Dust properties

| Property | Value | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dust temperature at bright rings | B67: 24 K; B100: 15 K; B168: >6 K | Dust temperature, or lower limit, estimated from the continuum intensity at the main bright rings. | [@isella2018] |
| Dust extinction optical depth | B67: 0.64 ± 0.05; B100: 0.74 ± 0.05; B168: 0.1 ± 0.2 | Total dust extinction at the rings, including both absorption and scattering. | [@isella2018] |
| Dust scattering optical depth | B67: ~0.1–0.2; B100: ~0.2–0.4 | Estimated contribution of dust scattering in the main bright rings. | [@isella2018] |
| Radial extent of the millimeter dust continuum | within 250 AU | Maximum distance from the star where the emission from large dust grains is clearly detected in the millimeter continuum. | [@isella2016] |
| Dust depletion factor in the gaps | inner gap: ~13 to >100; middle gap: ~7 to 70; outer gap: ~3.6 to 6 | Measure of how much the dust density decreases inside each gap compared with the surrounding regions; higher values indicate more strongly dust-depleted gaps. | [@isella2016] |

## Gas in the disk

According to the gas structure of HD 163296, the gaseous disk does not end where the millimeter dust continuum is observed, but instead extends much farther outward: “**The CO emission extends far beyond the outer edge of millimeter-wave continuum emission up to an angular distance of 5 6, or about 560 au, from the central star**” [@isella2018]. To understand how its geometry is observed, it is important to note that the CO-traced gas does not appear as a single layer, but as two warm emitting surfaces on the front and rear sides of the disk: “**The CO emission observed at intermediate velocities … shows a characteristic ‘dragonfly’ structure tracing both the front and rear warm CO-emitting layers of the disk**” [@isella2018]. The rear-side emission appears fainter because “**The emission coming from the rear of the disk is fainter than that from the front due to a combination of lower temperature of the emitting gas and absorption from the intervening material**” [@isella2018]. The separation between these two surfaces therefore makes it possible to infer the vertical structure of the gas, since “**the separation between the front and rear CO emission measures the vertical geometry of the CO-emitting surfaces**” [@isella2018]. In addition, the CO temperature decreases with distance from the star, reaching values “**as high as 120 K in the innermost disk regions and as low as 20 K at the disk outer edge**” [@isella2018]. Finally, the global motion of the gas follows an ordered rotation dominated by stellar gravity: “**The overall kinematics of the CO gas is well described by Keplerian rotation around a star with a mass of about 2.0 M⊙**” [@isella2018]. Unlike the dust, the gas does not trace prominent rings and gaps, but instead appears in a smoother way, since “**The maps of the 12CO, 13CO, and C18O J = 2 − 1 emission do not show such structures but reveal a change in the slope of the radial intensity profile across the positions of the dark rings in the continuum image**” [@isella2016]. This shows that what matters is not only how much gas is present, but also how it is redistributed relative to the dust, because “**we deduce that the gas-to-dust ratio varies across the disk and, in particular, it increases by at least a factor 5 within the inner dust gap compared to adjacent regions of the disk**” [@isella2016], suggesting that in this inner region the dust is more depleted than the gas.

## Gas properties

| Property | Value | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radial extent of the CO emission | ~560 au | Maximum distance from the star at which CO-traced gas is still clearly detected; it shows that the gaseous disk extends much farther out than the millimeter dust continuum. | [@isella2018] |
| Geometry of the CO-emitting layer | z<sub>CO</sub>(100 au) = 30 au; q = 0.5 | Describes the height of the warm CO-emitting surface above the disk midplane and how that height changes with radius; it summarizes the vertical structure of the observed gas. | [@isella2018] |
| Temperature range of the CO-traced gas | inner disk: up to 120 K; outer edge: down to 20 K | Approximate temperature of the gas detected in CO from the inner regions to the outer disk; it shows that the gas is hotter near the star and colder at larger radii. | [@isella2018] |
| Radial CO temperature profile | T<sub>CO</sub>(K) ≈ 87 − 0.14 (r/au), between 30 and 500 au | Empirical relation describing how the gas temperature decreases with distance from the star across a large fraction of the disk. | [@isella2018] |
| Systemic velocity | 5.8 km s<sup>−1</sup> | Reference velocity of the HD 163296 system inferred from the CO observations; it is used to describe the disk’s overall rotation pattern. | [@isella2018] |
| Gas kinematics | Keplerian rotation around a star of ~2.0 M☉ | Global dynamical behavior of the gas, consistent with a disk whose rotation is primarily governed by the gravity of the central star. | [@isella2018] |
| Radius of the Keplerian gaseous disk | ~550 AU | Approximate size of the gaseous disk orbiting the star; it indicates how far the gas remains part of the main rotating disk structure. | [@isella2016] |
| CO depletion in the gaps | inner gap: ΔCO1 = 0–2.5; middle gap: ΔCO2 = 3.5–70; outer gap: ΔCO3 = 1.8–6 | Measure of how strongly the CO-traced gas decreases inside each gap; higher values indicate a stronger reduction of gas in that region. | [@isella2016] |
| Gas-to-dust ratio | ~40 at 100 AU; >1000 for r > 250 AU; at least 5 times higher inside the inner gap | Ratio between gaseous material and dust in different parts of the disk; it shows that gas and dust are not distributed in the same way, and that some regions are relatively more depleted in dust than in gas. | [@isella2016] |
| Gas aspect ratio | h = 0.05 at 60 AU; 0.065 at 100 AU; 0.07 at 160 AU | Ratio between the thickness of the gaseous disk and its radius; it helps describe how vertically extended or “flared” the gas is in different regions of the disk. | [@isella2016] |

## Evidence of planet formation

The architecture of HD 163296, particularly its gaps and rings, is linked to real perturbations in the disk gas rather than to changes in dust opacity alone, as shown by the fact that “The recent detection of deviations from Keplerian rotation at the location of the gaps and rings by Teague et al. (2018) confirms that these structures correspond to variations in the gas pressure (i.e., variations in the gas density and/or temperature), as opposed to radial changes in the dust opacity” [@isella2018]. In this context, comparisons between the observations and planet–disk interaction models suggest that several of these gaps may have been carved by planets, since “The comparison between the observations and planet–disk interaction models indicates that the density gaps might have been carved by planets with masses between 0.5 and 1 MJ orbiting at 48, 86, and 131 au from the central star” [@isella2018]. The paper also shows that this evidence does not rely on a single scenario, because under very low-viscosity conditions “the observed multiple ring structure may also result from the gravitational interaction with a single planet that is less massive than Saturn at 100 au orbital radius” [@isella2018]. In addition, substructures such as the crescent observed inside D45 reinforce this interpretation, since “features similar to the crescent observed inside the D45 gap (inset b of Figure 1) are naturally produced by the planet–disk interaction whenever the disk viscosity is low (α < 10−3) and the planet mass is above about 0.1 MJ” [@isella2018]. Finally, the continuum asymmetries are not interpreted merely as morphological details, but as further support for this hypothesis, because “these asymmetries trace local variations in the dust densities and support the hypothesis that the HD 163296 disk is shaped by the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” [@isella2018]. This interpretation is extended by recent dynamical studies, in which HD 163296 “has been proposed to host at least four giant planets on wide orbits as well as a massive planetesimal disc” [@polychroni2025], so that the evidence for planet formation is not limited to the observed morphology, but also includes its consequences for the solid material in the system. Within that framework, “in our simulations the formation of giant planets in the HD 163296 system creates a large population of dynamically excited planetesimals, the majority of which originate from beyond the CO snowline” [@polychroni2025], and “HD 163296’s giant planets between 50 and 260 au are capable of delivering large amounts of planetesimals inwards of 40 au” [@polychroni2025], suggesting that the presence of young giant planets may not only sculpt rings and gaps, but also redistribute material toward the inner regions of the system.

## Physical interpretation of HD 163296

HD 163296 can be physically interpreted as a disk in which dust is not distributed uniformly, but instead tends to accumulate in specific regions of the gas. In particular, “**Dullemond et al. (2018) finds that many of the continuum rings revealed by DSHARP observations, including the rings B67 and B100 of the HD 163296 disk, have a radial width that is narrower than the estimated pressure scale height of the gaseous disk, and conclude that this is strong evidence that these rings have formed by radial migration of dust particles toward local maxima of the gas pressure**” [@isella2018]. This suggests that the rings are not simply patterns seen in the image, but regions where dust has migrated and accumulated because the local gas pressure favors it. At the same time, this architecture develops within a thin disk, and the outer region is not perfectly uniform, since “**The disc is shown to be thin … Significant modulation of the intensity of the outer ring emission is revealed and discussed**” [@diep2019]. This complexity cannot be explained by smooth dust trapping alone, because “**the observed asymmetries in the dust continuum emission corroborate the hypothesis that the complex structure of the HD 163296 disk is the result of the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets**” [@isella2018]. Meanwhile, the gas preserves an overall ordered motion, given that “**The three line emissions display remarkably similar kinematics dominated by Keplerian rotation. An upper limit of 9% of the rotation velocity has been placed on a possible in-fall velocity**” [@diep2019]. Taken together, these results suggest a system in which the rings arise from dust concentration at gas-pressure maxima, while the more complex structures, such as asymmetries and brightness modulations, point to active interaction between the disk and possible forming planets.

## Activity

Coming soon

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
